text:
Admission Date:  2019-04-25       Discharge Date:  2009-10-22

Date of Birth:   1982-04-02       Sex:  F

Service:  Acove

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:  Ms Martinez is a 64 year old
female with a past medical history of acquired Factor Price
inhibitor and therefore she suffers from recurrent bleeding.
The patient was recently admitted in 05 when she was
first diagnosed with Factor 09 inhibitor with a severe left
thigh hematoma which required subsequent fasciotomy.  Her
hospital course at that time was complicated by epistaxis and
a shoulder hematoma.  The patient was then readmitted to Berkshire Medical Center - Hillcrest Campus in 21 with a hemorrhoidal
bleed.  During both admissions she required activated Factor
VII transfusions.  On the day of admission, the patient
presented with a new left thigh hematoma.  Since discharge
from the Deborah Heart And Lung Center on 1976-04-05, she has been continued on Cyclophosphamide 150 mg q.d.
and Prednisone 60 mg q.d. to help quiet the auto-immune
Factor [**Year (4 digits) 7060**] inhibitor.  The patient states that she has no
recollection of thigh trauma or injury but just noticed on
the day prior to admission the evidence of bruising.  Over
the day of admission she developed more pain in the leg and
noted increase in the amount of bruising and called her
physician who told her to come to the hospital.  On admission
the patient was complaining of severe left leg pain but
denied bright red blood per rectum, melena, hematemesis or
nausea or vomiting.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:  1.  Factor [**Year (4 digits) 7060**] inhibitor acquired,
likely related to rheumatoid arthritis, suffering from
recurrent bleeding as stated above, initially diagnosed in
1972-08-20 when she presented with left thigh bruising and
hematoma.  She was hospitalized from 2013-11-06 to
2005-05-25 for complications of this left thigh hematoma
after an intertrochanteric steroid injection.  The patient
required fasciotomy, multiple Intensive Care Unit admissions
from the floor to receive q two hour Factor VII A infusions.
The patient was transitioned from Methotrexate to
Cyclophosphamide and Prednisone at this time and eventually
discontinued to home, off of Factor VII A but then was
readmitted from 04-07 to 1976-04-05 for bright red
blood per rectum, again requiring Factor VII A transfusions.
2.  Rheumatoid arthritis.  3.  Antiphospholipid antibodies
but no history of clotting.  4.  Hypertension.  5.  History
of diverticulitis.

ALLERGIES:  The patient states no known drug allergies.

MEDICATIONS ON ADMISSION:  1.  Cyclophosphamide 150 mg q.d.;
2.  Prednisone 50 mg q.d.; 3.  Bactrim double-strength one
tablet q. Monday; 4.  Propranolol 40 mg t.i.d.; 5.
Atorvastatin 10 q.d.; 6.  Calcium carbonate 500 t.i.d.; 7.
Alendronate 5 mg q.d.; 8.  Pantoprazole 40 mg q.d.; 9.
Colace 100 mg b.i.d.; 10. Iron 325 mg q.d.; 11. Folate one
q.d.; 12. Dulcolax 10 q.d.; 13. Hydrocortisone to external
hemorrhoids q.d.; 14. Senna one tablet q.h.s.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:  On admission the patient was slightly
febrile with a temperature of 100.1, blood pressure ranged
from 85/33 to 130/80, pulse 118, respiratory rate 18,
saturation 96 to 99% on room air.  In general the patient was
in no apparent distress but was complaining of discomfort in
her left leg.  Head, eyes, ears, nose and throat, pupils
equal, round and reactive to light and accommodation.
Extraocular muscles intact.  Oropharynx unremarkable.  Neck
supple, no jugulovenous distension or lymphadenopathy.  Heart
regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs, rubs or gallops.  Lungs
clear to auscultation.  Abdomen, soft, nontender,
nondistended, positive bowel sounds, no hepatosplenomegaly.
Old ecchymoses on left lower flank.  No costovertebral angle
tenderness.  Extremities, no cyanosis, clubbing or edema.
Large dark ecchymoses involving most of the posterior left
thigh and the anterior knee, 2+ pedal pulses bilaterally.
Old ecchymoses around the fasciotomy site.  The left thigh
was warm and tender to touch.  Neurological, alert and
oriented times three.  Cranial nerves II through XII are
intact.  Sensation, left thigh intact.

LABORATORY DATA:  Hematocrit 28.9 which the patient's
baseline is 30 to 33, platelet count 208, white count 10,
hemoglobin 9.8.  Differential on white count 77% neutrophils,
0 bands, 16% lymphocytes, 4 monocytes, 0 basophils, 0
eosinophils, 0 atypicals, 1 meta, 2 myelocytes.  Creatinine
0.6.  On 2014-09-06, Factor [**Year (4 digits) 7060**] was 6, normal being 50
to 150.  On the day of admission, 06-18, Factor 09 was
21.  Factor 09 inhibitor was also measured on admission,
was pending.  Last measurement was 7.8 units/ml.  Urinalysis
was negative.

IMPRESSION:  This is a 64 year old female with a history of
rheumatoid arthritis and a quiet Factor 09 inhibitor
diagnosed earlier this year who presents with her second left
thigh hematoma.  The patient's bleeding in the absence of
trauma was presumed to be due to her Factor 09 inhibitor,
despite her relatively elevated Factor Robert Miller level and PTT
with respect to her prior hospital values.

Hematology/Oncology was consulted immediately and recommended
the patient be treated with Recombinant Factor VII A every 4
hours, she was to be given an infusion of 4800 units.   Given
that the current bleeding appears out of proportion to the
patient's relatively improved Factor Robert Miller level on PTT it was
instead decided to follow the patient's hematocrit q. 6 to 8
hours and to follow the size of her hematoma and her distal
pulses.

HOSPITAL COURSE:  On the evening of admission, the patient's
blood pressure was running low with systolics running
anywhere from 80 to 100 and her heartrate was relatively
high, running from 100 to 110.  The elevated heartrate was
likely a combination of pain related to the left thigh
bleeding but also with the hypotension likely reflected
volume depletion.  It was decided as above to check serial
hematocrits.  The patient's repeat hematocrit on the evening
of admission had dropped from 28.9 to 25.1.  As the patient
was symptomatic with hypotension and some lightheadedness it
was decided to transfuse the patient with 1 unit of packed
red cells.  She received this transfusion without
complications.  She was treated with small intravenous fluid
boluses until the blood was prepared.  It was also decided in
light of the patient's hypotension to hold the Propranolol.
The patient was continued on her p.o. Prednisone and
Cyclophosphamide which is both for her rheumatoid arthritis
and acquired Factor Robert Miller inhibitor, she was initially given
as stated above Factor VII, q. 4 hours, however, during the
evening of her admission as her leg became more painful, and
as the hematoma was appearing to expand it was decided to
increase the interval between Factor VII A and she was
changed to q. 2 hour Factor VII A transfusions at midnight.
On the morning of hospital day #2 the patient's hematocrit
had stabilized at 27.2 after a unit of packed red cells.  Her
thigh hematoma was increasing in size among the medial aspect
and along the posterior thigh and her leg was quite tender.
Therefore it was decided to continue the q. 2 hour Factor VII
A transfusions that were initiated the night before.  In
order to do this, the patient had to be temporarily
transferred to the Texas Health Huguley Hospital as these transfusions could not be
administered on the floor q. 2 hours.  The patient continued
to be normotensive even though the hematoma on her leg was
expanding, there were no signs of compartment syndrome.  It
was decided to send blood cultures and urine cultures with
the patient's hypotension and lowgrade temperature to be sure
there was no infectious etiology causing sepsis.  The patient
was kept on q. 2 hour Factor VII A transfusions over 12-19
and 04-26.  Her hematocrit remained relatively stable and
she required no further blood transfusions.  On 07-25 it
was decided to increase the Factor VII A transfusion into q.
4 hours as her hematocrit was stable and there were no signs
of bleeding.  She was continued on her Cyclophosphamide and
Prednisone.  Later in the day on 07-25, the patient was
transferred back to the Acove team to the regular medicine
floor.  It was decided to check her hematocrit q. 12 hours
and to continue Factor VII A infusions about every six hours
at this point.  On the morning of 05-12, the patient's
hematocrit was again stable at 30.1.  The patient had much
less pain in the left leg.  As her hematocrit had now been
stable for two days it was decided to stop the Factor VII A
transfusions and to follow her thigh examination and check
her hematocrit in the morning.  The next morning, 09-28,
the patient's hematocrit was still stable at 31.  The patient
was ambulating without difficulty and only in moderate pain.
Her thigh examination was stable.  It was decided to
discontinue the patient to home.  She was to follow up with
her hematologist/oncologist. Dr. Allen in clinic on King,
02-24, at 11 AM.

MEDICATIONS ON DISCHARGE:  Her discharge medications were not
changed from her admission medications.  It was considered to
start Ambicor prior to discharge but it was decided to hold
off on this and have Dr. Allen decide when to start this
medication if at all.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:
1.  Acquired Factor Oconnor inhibitor
2.  Rheumatoid arthritis




                            Kaitlin Young, M.D.

Dictated By:Snyder
